hook_text_english: Spanish conquistadors called platinum 'little silver' thinking it hadn't ripened into gold
full_text_english:
Encountering the metal in the New World in the sixteenth century, Spanish conquistadors assumed that the metal was simply gold that hadn’t yet ripened- they gave it the derisive name “platina” meaning “little silver.”

#history #thehistoryguy #elements
body_summary_english: History of platinum naming by Spanish conquistadors in the sixteenth century. Directs viewers to The History Guy merchandise shop.
comments: That cat looks huge.
I was dredging for gold last summer and found a small silvery half gram nugget.  One XRF zap later, turned out to be a natural platinum nugget!  Was a pretty cool find.

You know I didn’t know anything about Platinum before.
Thanks!
In the 1970s when the  aerospace Research and Development group I was part of needed some platinum wire, it was delivered by a Brinks truck. Worth more than gold at that time.
An odd aside about platinum, one of the first chemotherapy drugs is a platinum compound 'Cisplat' and was discovered accidentally.

We live in the Black Hills and used to pan for gold from time to time.

One day I had a pan loaded with some diggings and to my surprise a long, narrow silver “nugget” showed up in the pan.

I thought it was a bit of foil or other trash at first but eventually I confirmed that it is platinum.  Only time I ever found any and no, I’m not saying where it was! 😊

During the Cariboo gold rush miners on the Tulameen river were annoyed by the shiny silver metal they were pulling alongside gold.  They would pull it out and throw it away.  
Turns out it was platinum.

I worked for Owens Corning Fiberglass for a time and got a tour of one of their fiberglass plants. The liquid glass is drawn out of a "swimming pool" sized furnace tank through platinum 'spinets' to form the very fine glass fiber. The spinet is a >< 2" x 1" plate with tiny holes made entirely of platinum.

Something I constantly hear about and I'm embarrassed to say I actually thought 'White Gold' and 'Platinum' were the same thing. Not that I actually 'put thought into this' and that's why we have The History Guy to get it straightened out for us. 
'The History Guy' is the 'Platinum' of Yuotube btw.. the very very best channel!

6:40 "Sometimes history provides clues as to what has been lost, and reminds us that more history has been forgotten than remembered."
- Lance (a.k.a. THG)
Thank you Pocky & THG
Pocky is beautiful. Thanks for the excellent video.
Thank you History Guy for this precious episode. 💰
Love the Cat and The History Guy.
This metal was used in my medical treatments for seven months.  It saved my life but the damage done by this metal was profound and took many years to heal my body from its effects
Nice to see your su-purr-visor.
"More history has been lost than remembered."
I like the slower narration, and have started listening to the history guy again.
For most of my life, the price of platinum was about double the price of gold. It is only in recent years that gold overtook it. Now it is fairly consistently the other way 'round.

Platinum is a key catalyst used in isomerization and reforming of naphtha into high octane gasoline. Without these processes the gasoline pool would be reduced by 30-50% and would have to rely heavily on alkylation, a more expensive product. As well, the primary biproduct of reforming is hydrogen. Without this, much more natural gas would have to be used to produce hydrogen for hydrotreating and hydrocracking. So without platinum catalysts gasoline would be much more expensive (say $2-3/gallon) and much dirtier when burned.
Great video, Lance! Perhaps consider doing a video on Titanium for your next elemental video.
Thank you for the video
When living in Japan in the previous century, I was surprised platinum was more commonly used than gold for wedding rings~
Mr Pocky looks suitably regal for your show
Love your videos

Thank you for the lesson.
They deserve a platinum award.
The ingenuity of the ancients never ceases to amaze me
Thanks History Guy
Thank you for sharing!😊
Would love to see your coin collection !
I just calculated that the current value of all the platinum that’s ever been mined in history is $625 billion. That’s really not a lot of money when you think about it.
Pocky is obviously riveted.
Platinum is incredibly hard to work with.
My home language is Afrikaans (literally meaning African) spoken by European and mixed race South Africans call Platinum also Platina. The same pronunciation the Spanish use for Platinum.
The thumbnail of Lady Liberty looks like she's thinking, "HEAVY SIGH. Meh... whatEVER..." 😆

Fun episode. Titanium also was r regarded as something to overcome. It was in the way of mining iron and refining it. However in WWII it was needed so the US government put a rail line through the mountains to the High Peaks region of the Adirondack Mountains. Tawaws twice abandoned is where Teddy was when he was VP when he was told he was actually now the president. That building stands in poor repair and a Chinese owned conservancy owns the land all around this place last I knew. Sadly NL Industries pulled out even though they had a 100 year plan for the mine site. Newcome NY now it's only fed by tourism. They moved an entire town, buildings and all.... Fantastic story
Great video, educational and easy to follow. Also, lovely, beautiful kitty. Love him/her
Next video: Gold-Pressed Latinum
One of the first uses was for flint lock guns for flash blows parts because it was resistant to heat and corrosion

title: The Strange Story of Platinum
caption:
Encountering the metal in the New World in the sixteenth century, Spanish conquistadors assumed that the metal was simply gold that hadn’t yet ripened- they gave it the derisive name “platina” meaning “little silver.”

#history #thehistoryguy #elements
transcript: The mere word platinum conjures up the image of the very best. In the music industry, a platinum album has gotten a million sales, double that of a mere gold album. Platinum credit cards get the best benefits. Heck, platinum membership in anything always represents the highest tier. Oddly, platinum seems to have become the gold standard of standards. I guess that shouldn't be a surprise. Platinum is 30 times rarer than gold in the earth's crust. But the value of platinum was not always recognized. When Spanish concistadors came upon platinum in the rivers of the new world, they just assumed that it was merely gold that hadn't yet ripened. They gave it the derisive name platina, little silver. It's history that deserves to be remembered. Platinum isn't just rare in that it is uncommon in the Earth's crust. It is very difficult to get to. Platinum is one of the densest naturally occurring elements. The high density means that it is significantly heavier and more compact than everyday metals like iron or copper and denser than common precious metals even like gold and silver. A cube of platinum about 6 in square weighs approximately 165 lbs. And that means that the already rare element largely sank towards the Earth's core when the planet was forming, making the minable portion exceedingly rare. In fact, experts assume that most minable platinum arrived on the Earth after its formation via meteor impact. Only roughly 10,000 tons of platinum have been extracted in all of human history. Combined, that would produce a cube of only about 23 feet aside, a little more than 12,000 cubic feet, roughly the size of a goodiz living room. Platinum is also very difficult to manipulate and is very high melting point 3,214° F. The density, hardness, and extremely high melting point make it exceptionally challenging for jewelers to work with. That's why platinum doesn't for the most part appear in the ancient record. While some scholars have suggested that the Greek metal called electrum may have referred to platinum, that metal has been determined to be an alloy of gold and silver. Another suggestion is that a mention by Ply the elder of a heavy white substance found on the sands of Portugal and Galatia might have been referring to platinum, but most scholars agree that he was likely referencing tin. In fact, there's no real indication that ancient Greeks or Romans were aware of the element at all, both because it doesn't tend to naturally occur in the Mediterranean basin and they lack the furnace technology to melt it. Nor is there any sign that it was recognized in China or the Far East. There was however some use of platinum in the ancient world. At least a handful of items from ancient Egypt produced as early as 1,200 BC contained platinum. Allison Wy notes on the web page Julipedia that a 1901 analysis by French chemist Marcelyn Berto found that one of the silver colored metals used to decorate a 7th century BC object called the casket of thieves associated with Princess Chenepupet was native platinum mixed with small quantities of gold and aridium. However, with he explains, Berto believed the use of platinum by the Egyptians was not intentional but accidental as platinum was a natural component of the ores imported from Nubia. Jack M. Ogden noted in a 1971 edition of the Journal of Egyptian Archaeology that platinum and related metals are quite commonly associated with gold in aluvial deposits throughout the world. And it's generally assumed that ancient Egyptians were not knowingly working with platinum, but rather it was a natural impurity found in gold that they had imported. Given its characteristics, it would not be a surprise that the ancient peoples were generally unable to separate or deliberately manipulate platinum. But that is not universally the case. There are many examples of platinum jewelry from the Lalita culture, which thrived in what is the modern-day Esmeraldis region of Ecuador between 600 BC and 200 AD. While, as wa

hook_text_english: While Essex-class carriers grabbed headlines, Casablanca-class 'Jeep carriers' were the backbone
full_text_english:
While the massive Essex-class fleet carriers grabbed the headlines with their sprawling air groups and high-speed strikes, the "Jeep carriers" of the Casablanca class provided the indispensable backbone of Allied maritime operations.

#history #thehistoryguy #WWII
body_summary_english: Explores the overlooked role of Casablanca-class carriers in WWII operations. Promotes History Guy merchandise.
comments: In an age where AI Slop and Pseudoscience run rampant. It's cool seeing someone so dedicated to accurate historical information.

I was lucky and honored to design and build the Taffy3 memorial in San Diego. I met survivors of the battle. The man who spearheaded The project was on the st lo. Who had to jump in water. Mr John Ibe. Great episode.
History Cat is making history biscuits!

My uncle was on one of these, CVE-94 USS Lunga Point. I was able to handle pieces of metal from the suicide plane that unsuccessfully attempted to sink them off Iwo Jima (two more missed); sister CVE-95 Bismarck Sea was sank there by suicide planes.

The thing I remember vividly was my uncle talking about going through a typhoon on that ship. He said that during the typhoon they had every welder going 24 hours a day to keep the ship from coming apart.
We need an episode about the History Cat.
Listening to the Histoty Guy while watching the History Cat
My dad served on the Corregidor CVE-58. He spoke of  Kamikazes.
Yamato had greater displacement that all of Taffy 3 combined. A single turret weighted more than any single Fletcher class.

My father-in-law was an Avenger crewman assigned to VC-27 aboard CVE 76 "Savo Island".  They were assigned to Taffy 2 and participated in the Battle Off Samar, sinking a cruiser.  Although Taffy 1 and 2 were not under direct attack, they launched all their planes against the Japanese fleet - which helped to convince the Japanese that they were fighting a much larger group.  The Savo Island was also attacked by a kamikaze pilot on Jan 5.  They blinded the enemy pilot by shining the ship searchlight at the cockpit, resulting in a near miss.  Altogether they were involved in 5 major battles in a 6 month cruise.

My dad was the ships gunner (rank: CWO2) on the USS Windham Bay, CVE-92 for last two years of WWII, participating in the invasions of Iwo Jima and Okinawa. He was always scared the most of the Kamikazes. Every flattop was their favorite target.
My Father Kenneth McLaughlin WT/3 Served on and survived the sinking of CVE-73 USS Gambier Bay during the Battle off Samar Oct 25, 1944.

Thank you for dedicating a video to these often forgotten ships. They were known as being less able to survive an attack but the sailors served with distinction and bravery. Taffy 3 is most remembered but many others were involved in many other important and dangerous operations. A perfect example of history that deserves to be remembered.
That cat!!!🎉🎉😂
The story of Taffy 3 is one of the greatest legends of bravery in the history of man.
Honor to our fathers who fought however they could with whatever weapons they had!
The cat is a nice touch.
I love the fact that the guy who built the escort carriers that put the nail in the coffin of the Uboat threat in the Atlantic was named 'Kaiser'.
I was aware of escort carriers but didn't know much about them.  That has changed.  Thank you!

My father was the Flight Officer on the St. Lo, CVE-63. I had the privilege of meeting many of the survivors at the 50 Year reunion in 1994 at which my father was the senior surviving officer. I now live in Vancouver, Washington, and the shipyard where the St. Lo was built remains an industrial site. The slips where the final assembly and launch were done are still visible in the Columbia River.

Thanks so much, my Dad served on the USS Kit Kun Bay, which was part of Taffy 3.   He was a boilerman, down in the bottom of the ship.

Oddly enough, the ship was launched on November 8, 1944.  I was born November 8, 1948.  I wound up living in Vancouver and had a 30 year career on the Vancouver Fire Department.  The vestiges of the Kaiser ship yard are pretty much gone, it is mostly an industrial park now.  Thanks again for the video.
The men who served on these ships are truly heroes. Thank you.
My grandfather served on CVE-87 Steamer Bay. I have some of the letters he wrote my grandmother from that period.
13:50 Contextual swearing is always hilarious when someone who doesn’t swear outside of reading an account has to

A neighbor and old family friend was an officer aboard a CVE during WWII, serving in the Atlantic on antisubmarine duty.  He said life on these ships could be dangerous even when not under attack.  In one instance he was standing on the edge of the deck chatting with a fellow officer when an aircraft unexpectedly crash-landed, sending pieces flying in all directions.  Issac dove into the gun sponson just below him, but the other man did not react fast enough and was decapitated.
Losing Gambier Bay, Johnston, and Samuel B. Roberts to turn the Yamato around was a worthy loss. There's no real question that was still a strategic victory.
90% of the Casablanca class survived the war.  It was not a death sentence to be assigned to one.

My father served aboard both USS Block Islands, CVE-21 & CVE-106. The first one requisitioned an Army Jeep from the Dock side in Norfolk, VA. Also it is the only US Aircraft Carrier to be sunk in the Atlantic Ocean during the War.
That cat knows meow about history than your average dog.
I love watching the happy cat. Such a happy cat.
The Sten guns of the air fleet arm. Not the best ships, but the ships we needed.
USS St. Lo was originally named USS Midway. It was sunk 15 days after the name change.
CVE's, their building was an unmatched achievement. Thankfully we have never needed to exceed that...

My father was on CVE-103  USS Roi.  He joined the Navy at 17, and was in training when the war ended.  But he was on the Roi for about a year for Magic Carpet. I remember stories of his time aboard.  Since he was never involved in combat, he didn't have any issue talking about it.   I had an older uncle on the USS Mugford (DD-105) who was at Pearl Harbor on the 7th.  He never talked about any of his wartime experiences.  Thank you for a good video about the Casablanca class.
I can see several stories involving the Taffys vs the Japanese central fleet as well as some individual members of this class deserving their own episode.
Thank-You , for featuring Baby Flat tops!😊
Having more Carriers is better then having no carriers.
That is a cool stat. 1 CVE per week
You can still see today , the 12 slipways of the Kaiser Vancouver shipyard., rhat built the 50 Casablanca Class Escorts, Liberty ships and LSTs.

My grandfather served on CVE91 the USS Mackasar Straight. He also served on CV3 the USS Saratoga. He went on for a successful career. He switched branches and ended up retiring from the USCG as a Captain!
I am a long time resident of Vancouver Wa where some of the Kaiser shipyards were located. Sadly nothing really exists anymore of them.

While the massive Essex-class fleet carriers grabbed the headlines with their sprawling air groups and high-speed strikes, the "Jeep carriers" of the Casablanca class provided the indispensable backbone of Allied maritime operations.

#history #thehistoryguy #WWII
transcript: Doris Dorie Miller was a mess attendant, second class, aboard the battleship USS West Virginia on December 7th, 1941 in Pearl Harbor. His actions that day were legendary. He helped carry many of his crew members to safety, including his captain. And then he took control of an anti-aircraft gun despite having no training in the weapon and defended his ship, shooting down at least one Japanese aircraft. He became a a national hero overnight. He became the first black sailor to be awarded the Navy's highest award for valor in the face of the enemy, the Navy Cross. He was nominated for a Medal of Honor. Much less remembered was his death just 2 years later aboard the escort carrier USS Liscome Bay. Miller's death represented the risks faced by the young men who served aboard the ships that were derisively called Kaiser's coffins. The the fleet carriers, the Essex carriers, grabbed all the headlines with their sprawling air groups and their fast attacks, but the the small carriers, the jeep carriers of the Casablanca class, they were the indispensable backbone of Allied naval operations. The battle in which Dorie Miller became a hero put the US into a difficult position regarding naval aviation. In 1941, the US had only seven fleet aircraft carriers, a precious few even considering that none had been lost at Pearl Harbor. More were being constructed, but fleet carriers took time to build, and their air groups required training. There was plenty of demand, but one in particular would obviously draw the Navy's attention. Convoys crossing the Atlantic were vulnerable to the wolf packs of Admiral Karl Dönitz's U-boats through what was called the black pit or the mid-Atlantic gap, a vast stretch of roughly a thousand miles that was outside the reach of land-based aircraft. Losses in the gap were devastating, and the obvious solution, escorting convoys with aircraft carriers, concerned Rear Admiral William F. Halsey, the US Fleet's commander aircraft battle force, he worried that if his fleet carriers were immediately called to the Battle of the Atlantic, it would, Jeffrey D. Barlow explained in the 2013 edition of the US Naval Institute Press, "severely restrict both the training of naval aviators and the service's ability to transport army and navy aircraft to overseas bases." In December 1940, Halsey sent a memo to Admiral Harold R. Stark, Chief of Naval Operations, suggesting the procurement of suitable merchant vessels and their earliest conversion to auxiliary aircraft carriers. The following March, the US Navy acquired the 12,000-ton displacement cargo liner Mormacmail for experimental conversion into an aircraft carrier. Mormacmail was a type C3 cargo ship, one of nearly 240 built according to a design by the US Maritime Commission. Renamed USS Long Island, she was commissioned on June 2nd, 1941. Long Island saw important service, delivering some of the first planes to Guadalcanal, the famous Cactus Air Force, and training pilots. She also proved the concept, something that was cemented when in July of 1943 she was reclassified CVE-1. CVE was the Navy prefix for fixed-wing aircraft carriers, and E meant escort. More than four dozen CVEs were converted from C3 ships and later from Cimarron-class fleet oilers. These carriers provided vital service in numerous roles, transporting aircraft, fighting U-boats, and helping to close the mid-Atlantic gap. Although Navy Captain Calvin D. Durgin noted, "Due to their low speed and lack of protection and light armament, it is considered hazardous to employ a CVE group in operation where there's likely to be an effective enemy opposition." The problem was that even these conversions could not come quickly enough. James L. Knowles Jr. explained in Smithsonian Magazine in 2004, "As the war was slowly shifting in the Allies' favor, the increasing number of naval operations demanded more escort carriers. In 1943, industrialist Henry J. Kaiser presented a solution. Kaiser was a self-made man, the s

hook_text_english: Check out The History Guy for more History That Deserves to be Remembered
full_text_english: Check out The History Guy for more History That Deserves to be Remembered.
body_summary_english: Promotional message encouraging viewers to explore The History Guy channel for historical content.
comments: In 1888, Edward Bellamy wrote a book titled Looking Backwards.   It was about a man from 1888 who found himself in 1988.  One of the future predictions was that everyone used credit cards instead of money for payment. Bellamy was off by a few years.   This book would make an interesting podcast
I'm so confused how he made this leap. He forgot his wallet, so he thought of a card to keep in his wallet? Lol
Jokes on you! Over 75 years later and credit cards are a thing and people still leave their wallets at home with the credit cards in them LOL
As long as you don't mind being tracked every time you buy something, no matter what it is , you can pay more for the same thing.  They charge you to use it and they charge you to have it. 😢
The prediction won't quite come true. His plastic will be obsolete at the same time cash is. Already partially replaced with the cellphone and soon to be replaced completely with biometrics.
And the rest, they say, is history.
I'm just hung up on 200 friends and acquaintances.

I’m sitting here trying to remember when I got my first credit card - sometime in the early 70s I think?  These days I 99% use my debit card instead of writing a check or using cash!  I needed to write a check recently and I had almost forgotten how to do this!  I recently found a little bag filled with quarters - the ones that circulated from about 1999 to around 2005 representing all the states!  I had even forgotten about them - not going to use them though - still only worth 25 cents!!!
One of the people involved in the Diners Club was Matty Simmons, who would later go on to publish  National Lampoon.
* Schneider* I’m never surprised
Hopefully not

Scary thought.

There will be a day when an iris scan or a chip in the hand will make a plastic card obsolete.

And then later, the major banks got involved and took far more than their fair share! 

Charging outrageous interest to the customers, outrageous fees to the retailers, filing false late payments & fees, etc. 

Not to mention international theft rings, identity theft, etc. ...

Nope!  CASH will ALWAYS EXIST! IT HAS TO!!! 

Plastic is just not secure enough - fact!

This story would make more sense if he had forgotten to bring enough cash with him...in his wallet. You know, a wallet where you would carry your credit card, if you invented one, to use, when you didn't have cash.
And now I don’t even have to use a card. I click on my phone, it dings, and I get everything for free…until the credit card bill comes.
Amazing,  Alfred Bloomingdale was right.
This needs to be in Theatre Mode. I'm done! (1:26)
So far I never used my card, everything is bought in cash or via bank account. Looking at my peers, I am of a dieying species...
Another McNamara that ruined everything.
This makes no sense.
Yet usury itself is far older than plastic and is as old as Judaism itself.
Nice story...but completely fabricated as part of a larger plan.
title: Making Money Obsolete
caption: Check out The History Guy for more History That Deserves to be Remembered.
transcript: The modern credit card industry began on this date in 1950. In 1949, New York businessman Frank McNamara was having dinner at Major's Cabin Grill restaurant in New York City. When it came time to pay the bill, he realized that he'd forgotten his wallet. Fortunately for him, his wife was able to pay for the meal, but it was apparently the spark for a bigger idea. In 1950, McNamara and his partner Ralph Schneider put together their idea for the Diners Club. Customers would pay a $3 subscription fee to get a Diners Club card, which would be accepted at its inception at 28 restaurants and two hotels in New York. On May 13th, 1950, Diners Club officially launched its first charge card to a small group of 200 people, most of whom were personal friends and acquaintances. This date marks the beginning of the world's first independent payment card company. In its first year, 10,000 of New York's elite joined the program. In the same year, Alfred Bloomingdale, grandson of the founder of Bloomingdale's department store, started Diners Club in Los Angeles, a similar program. Later that year, the groups met and merged, with Bloomingdale becoming vice president. The card was immensely popular, becoming accepted in Canada, Cuba, and Mexico in 1953, and reaching a million members by 1959. Bloomingdale predicted that the day will come when the plastic card will make money obsolete.

commentsTranslated.3: As long as you don't mind being tracked every time you buy something, no matter what it is , you can pay more for the same thing.  They charge you to use it and they charge you to have it. 😢
commentsTranslated.7: I'm sitting here trying to remember when I got my first credit card - sometime in the early 70s I think?  These days I 99% use my debit card instead of writing a check or using cash!  I needed to write a check recently and I had almost forgotten how to do this!  I recently found a little bag filled with quarters - the ones that circulated from about 1999 to around 2005 representing all the states!  I had even forgotten about them - not going to use them though - still only worth 25 cents!!!
commentsTranslated.15: And now I don't even have to use a card. I click on my phone, it dings, and I get everything for free…until the credit card bill comes.

hook_text_english: Man-eating big cats are rare but prolific killers; studying history teaches us about coexistence
full_text_english:
Man-eating big cats are rare, but can be prolific killers.  Studying some of history's greatest struggles with nature’s creatures helps us to understand ourselves, and offers lessons as we continue to face the challenges of living in harmony with our environment.

#history #thehistoryguy #bigcats
body_summary_english: Examines historical conflicts with man-eating big cats and environmental harmony lessons. Promotes merchandise.
comments: Colonel Corbett has been one of my greatest heroes since shortly after I learnt to read.  Not only was he one of the greatest trackers and hunters recorded, but he was a pioneer of wildlife photography, making numerous films of tigers in the wilds of northern India.  He hunted the big man-eaters both for the sake of the Indian people, whom he loved, but also for the sake of the big cats which WEREN'T man-eaters, because he understood that if the man-eaters weren't killed, ALL the big cats would be killed.  But this wasn't his 'day job'.  He was the freight manager of Indian Railways, and in that role, he and his men held, for many years and perhaps still, held the record for the most freight shifted in a single day by a single railway.  During WW1 he was called in to manage the railways which moved supplies and passengers to and from the French ports to the British portion of the front line.  Altogether he was a very remarkable man.
If my cat can overpower me, without a doubt it will eat me, only reason im alive, someone's gotta open the cat food.
I read Jim Corrbett's "Man-Eaters of Kumaon" in high school and it was fascinating.

Jim Corbett was a memorable hunter but a fantastic author. The tales are gripping, the writing is excellent. Some of the best books in my lifetime of reading. It's not just what he did 100 years ago, but how well he describes what he did.
The history cat looks crabby but you know he's loving the attention.
Good cat,  good episode.
I haven't thought of The Ghost and the Darkness since the 20th century
I knew it!  I just knew it!  I knew that History Cat would be in a History Guy video about big cats.

Dear THG,
Meow.
Sincerely, The History Cat
Damn...that is one well dressed cat you have there! He looks like he approves of this video! Keep 'em coming!
I've escaped from at least 2 deadly beagles....

My friends son saved a 6 year old boy from a mountain lion attack 5 minutes from my home. In August 1998, a 6-year-old boy named Dante Swallow was attacked by a mountain lion while hiking with a day camp group at the Marshall Mountain ski area near Missoula, Montana. A 16-year-old camp counselor, Aaron Hall, fought off the animal to save the child, who survived with neck puncture wounds. I also had a young female mountain lion that had to be darted and relocated by a game warden next to my home a year before the attack. My 140lb German shepherd had treed it in the middle of the day.

The cat does not have that "you can put me down now" look today.
She must have known this was a cat episode.
It a shame he did not mention the tiger fatales in the Vietnam war

Jim Corbett inspired me to go to India, and visit his home in Naini tal. A wonderful journey. I read all his books as a schoolboy , truly inspirational, the man had nerves of steel. The fascination with Corbett endures, a new book has just been published, called, I beleive "Undiscovered Corbett" , not read it yet, but its on my "buy list"
I can only state that this is a timely subject, what with the sudden explosion of face-eating leopards and all ... bravo!

A little known story about Jim Corbett. He retired to Kenya, to a house called the Treetops, where a young lady visited him. There the lady got to know of her father's death. 

Jim Corbett described it as the lady who went up the tree a Princess but came down a Queen.

Queen Elizabeth 2 got to know of her father, KG6's death while she was with Jim Corbett.
My encounter with a cougar didn't go well. Although I was not eaten, she ate my money voraciously though .
I remember seeing somewhere that the big cat that is genetically closest to the house cat is the tiger.
Thank you THG & History Cat
Beautiful kitty!❤😊
One of the best channels on YouTube! ❤

'"True"; "Argosy"; "Outdoor Life"; & "Sports Afield" were monthly magazines my grand dad kept stacked in his basement to provide my older brother & myself an ample fodder of accounts of people killed & then eaten by wild animals.... Ah,  those were the days! Thank you, sir, for your delightful renderings.
well done / your cuddle buddy was a bis assistant!
I love History Cat and this video.  😊

As one who loves both this YouTube channel as well as wild big cats, my congratulations and thanks for this thoroughly balanced and researched episode. Then again, every episode is thoroughly balanced, researched, and presented.
So many lessons are contained in this glimpse of history.
The real problem is humans that are too stupid. to leave these magnificent animals alone.

When I lived in San Diego, CA, from the mid 2000s to the early 2010s, there were reports in the news about cougar attacks, primarily on joggers and bikers in the foothills surrounding San Diego. Most did not end in deaths, but frequently their injuries were severe.
I didn't think Pocky would eat _my_ face when I voted for the Pocky Ate My Face Party
I just love the expression on your Moggie's face!

Lance you may have been told this already, but a fantastic channel, Selva Lore,  with readings of Jim Corbett’s books makes for wonderful listening in the evening on your porch where most of us only have to fear mosquitos in this day. Love your channel and enjoying your comments on History Channel’s shows. Best wishes, Chris in Missouri.

I have been listening to these man-eater stories by Jim Corbett and Ken Anderson for the past few weeks, on the channel Selva Lore here on youtube. They are wonderful to fall asleep too, lol. What a fortunate time for you to upload this! The splendid writing by these exceptionally brave and well educated men makes me wish to improve my writing, and to be in my forest with the wild beasts. In Oregon the worst thing to run into is a cougar wolf or bear, no leopards or tigers😢
Who else thought of the Hall and Oats song "Maneater" after seeing this title
The History Cat looks extremely vicious and is most definitely a Man hunter😊
Great video! I read all of Corbet's books years ago and Maneaters of Tsavo shortly after. All great reads.
Hey History Guy! Please do an episode about the man eating horses!
Mountain lions and brown bears are the only 2 animals I fear encountering out of all the animals of North America. They are both ruthless killers.

Podcasters interacting with their pets has become commonplace.

Can't wait for a farmster to have his cow or pig on the show.
I just finished the Jim Corbett omnibus a few months back, so seeing this video was incredible!
title: The Leopard of Rudraprayag and Other Man-Eaters

#history #thehistoryguy #bigcats
transcript: We tend to think of history as something that occurs between humans. But in fact, some of the greatest struggles in history have been between man and his environment. As our civilization has grown, we continually sought to tame, to exploit, and to protect ourselves from nature and all of its creatures. And that has become even more difficult as our civilization continues to move further into their habitats. In many ways, a relationship with nature's creatures is a definition of humanity itself. So many of our myths, our lore, our legend are based around the eternal struggle with the unknown. And so learning about some of history's greatest difficulties in dealing with nature's creatures can teach us a lot about ourselves and maybe teach us some lessons as we continue to try to live in harmony with our environment. While its name may not be familiar to you, the Chowoet Tigris was one of the most prolific of the so-called maneaters in recorded history. She started killing in Nepal sometime around 1903. As villagers started disappearing, hunters were dispatched, but found the animal difficult to track. In fact, maneaters can be particularly clever. This tiger, or rather as it was later determined, tigress could be quite bold, attacking from ambush to pull a person from a crowd and yet seemed to know to avoid people who were armed. As she continued to evade hunters, and the death toll rose, the government eventually dispatched soldiers of the Nepalese army, who still failed to catch the animal, but whose widespread action seems to have driven her to crossover into India. By then, she had killed more than 200 people in Nepal. Tigers are among the largest of the world's great cats. Males can weigh over 700 lb or 325 kg. They attack from ambush, typically from the side or behind, and are capable of bringing down an adult Indian rhinoceros. Conflicts between humans and tigers are not uncommon. Patrick Greenfield noted in the Guardian in 2025 that an outbreak of African swine flu among wild boars, a favored prey, led to a spike of tiger attacks on humans last winter. Greenfield reports that some regions have seen a 1,000% increase in human tiger conflict incidents as African swine fever has spread. One expert told the Guardian, "The tigers are hungry." Still, the Siberian tigers affected are rarely dangerous, and only seven human deaths were attributed to them between 2010 and 2024. But even though tigers do not prefer humans as prey, and man-eating tigers are rare, it is estimated that tigers have killed as many as a million people over the last five centuries. After being driven into India, the Tigress settled into the remote Champawit district where she became even more dangerous and territorial. She had learned hunter's behavior and continued to evade them. She became bold enough to attack villages in the middle of the day. Villagers refused to go out and boarded up their windows, convinced that rather than a tiger, they were being hunted by demons. In 1906, an administrator invited a noted game hunter named Jim Corbett to hunt the Chowo Tigris. Corbett was an officer in the British Indian Army who had been raised in India and was a famously skilled hunter and crackshot. After setting the condition that all bounties be removed and that other hunters and soldiers be removed from the hunt, he hunted down and killed the tigress in 1907. Her fatal mistake was boldly killing a 16-year-old girl, her last victim, and thus leaving a trail that Corbett was able to follow. After finding the general location where the tiger had been hiding, he organized 300 villagers to do a beat the next day. Walking in line through the forest, making noise, driving the tigress out where Corbett could kill her. Upon examination, the tigress is found to have lost the upper and lower canine teeth on the right side of her mouth, apparently from a hunter's bullet. The damage had made it difficult for her to catch regular prey, and that was likely both what had d
